message.content: Masquerading: Right-to-Left Override (RTLO) is a technique where adversaries exploit the RTLO Unicode character (U+202E) to disguise file names, making them appear benign. This non-printing character reverses the text that follows it, misleading users and security tools. For instance, a malicious executable might appear as a harmless document. Commonly used in spearphishing attacks, this technique can deceive both end users and defenders, leading to the execution of malicious files. Notable groups like BlackTech, BRONZE BUTLER, Ferocious Kitten, Ke3chang, and Scarlet Mimic have employed RTLO to obfuscate filenames in their campaigns. Mitigation strategies include educating users about this tactic, implementing robust email filtering, and using security tools that can detect and render RTLO characters correctly. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help in identifying and preventing such obfuscation attempts.
